prompt: During a pre-trip inspection, the air compressor governor typically cuts out (stops pumping) at about which pressure range?

options.text: Around 120–140 psi
Around 40–60 psi
Around 60–80 psi
Around 180–200 psi

explanation: Most air-brake systems cut out near 120–140 psi so the compressor does not over-pressurize the tanks. Drivers must know cut-in/cut-out ranges for safe operation.

distractorExplanations.b: 40–60 psi is far below normal operating pressure and would indicate a serious air-system problem.
distractorExplanations.c: 60–80 psi is closer to a low-air warning area, not the cut-out range.
distractorExplanations.d: 180–200 psi exceeds typical governor cut-out and could indicate a malfunctioning governor.

prompt: What is the primary purpose of a slack adjuster on an S-cam air brake?

options.text: To increase air tank capacity
To maintain proper brake-chamber pushrod travel / brake adjustment
To cool the brake drums
To filter moisture from the air lines

explanation: Slack adjusters convert chamber pushrod force into cam rotation and must be adjusted (manual or automatic) so pushrod travel stays within limits.

distractorExplanations.a: Air tank capacity is fixed by reservoir design, not slack adjusters.
distractorExplanations.c: Cooling is not the function of a slack adjuster.
distractorExplanations.d: Air dryers and drain valves manage moisture, not slack adjusters.

prompt: If the low air pressure warning activates while driving, what should you do first?

options.text: Continue to the next exit at highway speed
Pump the brake pedal rapidly to rebuild pressure
Stop safely as soon as possible and park until the problem is fixed
Disable the warning light and finish the trip

explanation: Low-air warning means braking capability is compromised. Stop safely, secure the vehicle, and do not continue until air pressure is restored and the defect is corrected.

distractorExplanations.a: Continuing at highway speed risks brake failure.
distractorExplanations.b: Pumping the pedal wastes remaining air and is not a fix.
distractorExplanations.d: Ignoring a low-air warning is unsafe and illegal.

prompt: On a dual air brake system, why are there typically two separate air tanks/circuits?

options.text: To double fuel economy
To power only the trailer ABS
To eliminate the need for a parking brake
So one circuit can still provide braking if the other fails

explanation: Dual systems isolate front and rear (or primary/secondary) circuits so a leak in one does not leave the vehicle with zero service brakes.

distractorExplanations.a: Air circuits do not improve fuel economy.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS uses the air system but dual tanks are for redundancy, not ABS alone.
distractorExplanations.c: Parking brakes (spring brakes) are still required.

prompt: What does an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) primarily help a driver do?

options.text: Maintain steering control during hard braking on slippery surfaces
Shorten stopping distance on dry pavement in all cases
Eliminate the need for following distance
Automatically downshift the transmission

explanation: ABS prevents wheel lockup so the driver can steer while braking hard; it does not guarantee shorter stops on every surface.

distractorExplanations.b: ABS may not shorten stops on dry pavement versus properly applied non-ABS brakes.
distractorExplanations.c: Following distance is still required with ABS.
distractorExplanations.d: ABS does not control transmission shifting.

prompt: During an en-route inspection of cargo, what is a key reason to check tire pressure when tires are cool if possible?

options.text: Cool tires always have zero pressure
Heat from driving raises pressure and can mask underinflation
CDL rules forbid checking hot tires
Tire pressure only matters for passenger cars

explanation: Heat increases gauge pressure; checking after long runs can hide underinflation. Underinflated tires overheat and can fail.

distractorExplanations.a: Cool tires still hold rated pressure when properly inflated.
distractorExplanations.c: Hot tires can be checked cautiously; cool checks are preferred for accuracy.
distractorExplanations.d: Commercial tires require proper inflation just as much as car tires.

prompt: What is the function of the trailer air supply (red) glad hand / supply line?

options.text: To provide only electrical power to lights
To steer the trailer axles
To charge the trailer air tanks and control the trailer emergency/parking brakes
To cool the trailer refrigeration unit

explanation: The supply (emergency) line charges trailer reservoirs and, if disconnected or loses pressure, applies trailer emergency brakes.

distractorExplanations.a: Electrical power uses the 7-way cable, not the red air line.
distractorExplanations.b: Steering is not controlled by glad hands.
distractorExplanations.d: Reefer units have their own power/fuel systems.

prompt: A driver notices the water temperature gauge climbing into the danger zone. Which action is most appropriate?

options.text: Shut off the engine immediately while still in traffic
Increase speed to force more air through the radiator
Cover the radiator to trap heat
Pull off safely, idle to cool if safe, and investigate before continuing

explanation: Overheating requires a safe stop and diagnosis (coolant, belts, debris). Stopping in traffic lanes or forcing the engine harder worsens risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Immediate shutdown in a travel lane creates a collision hazard; get clear first when possible.
distractorExplanations.b: Higher speed increases load and may worsen overheating.
distractorExplanations.c: Covering the radiator traps heat and damages the engine.

prompt: What is a common sign of a leaking or defective shock absorber?

options.text: Excessive bouncing after hitting a bump
Increased fuel tank capacity
Higher governed RPM
Automatic parking brake application

explanation: Worn shocks allow prolonged bouncing and poor tire contact, reducing control and increasing wear.

distractorExplanations.b: Shocks do not change fuel capacity.
distractorExplanations.c: Governed RPM is engine control, unrelated to shocks.
distractorExplanations.d: Parking brakes are spring-brake systems, not shock-related.

prompt: Why must air tanks be drained regularly on many air-brake vehicles?

options.text: To lower tire pressure
To remove water and oil that can freeze or damage valves
To recharge the batteries
To reset the odometer

explanation: Compressed air produces condensate; oil and water collect in tanks and can freeze lines or foul valves if not drained.

distractorExplanations.a: Draining tanks does not adjust tire pressure.
distractorExplanations.c: Batteries are electrical, not drained via air tanks.
distractorExplanations.d: Odometers are unrelated to air-tank drains.

prompt: During a pre-trip, oil pressure that stays near zero after the engine starts usually indicates:

options.text: Normal warm-up for turbocharged diesels
A serious lubrication problem that can destroy the engine quickly
That the coolant thermostat is stuck open
That DEF fluid needs topping off

explanation: Oil pressure must build promptly; near-zero pressure risks catastrophic engine damage.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Normal warm-up for turbocharged diesels
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: That the coolant thermostat is stuck open
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: That DEF fluid needs topping off

prompt: What is the main reason to check suspension components (springs, air bags, U-bolts) during inspection?

options.text: They only affect radio antenna mounting
Damaged suspension can cause poor control, uneven tire wear, and unsafe ride height
Suspension parts determine hours-of-service limits
They replace the need for a fifth-wheel check

explanation: Suspension defects compromise stability, tire contact, and cargo/vehicle control.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: They only affect radio antenna mounting
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Suspension parts determine hours-of-service limits
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: They replace the need for a fifth-wheel check

prompt: If the low air warning activates while driving, the safest immediate response is generally to:

options.text: Continue to the next rest area at highway speed
Ignore it if ABS lights are off
Bring the vehicle to a safe stop and diagnose before continuing
Pump the accelerator to rebuild pressure

explanation: Low air can lead to spring-brake application and loss of service brakes; stop safely and fix it.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Continue to the next rest area at highway speed
distractorExplanations.b: Incorrect: Ignore it if ABS lights are off
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Pump the accelerator to rebuild pressure

prompt: Why must windshield wipers and washers work properly on a CMV?

options.text: They are optional if headlights work
Clear forward vision is required for safe operation in rain, snow, and road spray
They only matter during annual inspections
They reduce fuel taxes

explanation: Impaired visibility is a major crash risk; wipers/washers are essential inspection items.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: They are optional if headlights work
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: They only matter during annual inspections
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: They reduce fuel taxes

prompt: A leaking air hose or gladhand connection on a combination vehicle can cause:

options.text: Improved trailer tracking
Loss of trailer brake air supply and potential service or emergency brake problems
Automatic increases in engine oil pressure
Higher DEF consumption only

explanation: Air leaks threaten brake supply pressure and can force emergency spring-brake application.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Improved trailer tracking
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Automatic increases in engine oil pressure
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Higher DEF consumption only

prompt: On dry pavement, a common rule of thumb for following distance for a heavy vehicle is at least:

options.text: One second for each 10 feet of vehicle length at speeds under 40 mph, plus extra at higher speeds
One car length total regardless of speed
Half a second behind any vehicle
Zero following distance if ABS is equipped

explanation: Commercial manuals teach length-based following time (e.g., 1 second per 10 ft under 40 mph) and more space at higher speeds or in poor conditions.

distractorExplanations.b: One car length is inadequate for trucks.
distractorExplanations.c: Half a second provides almost no reaction time.
distractorExplanations.d: ABS does not remove the need for space.

prompt: When making a right turn in a long combination vehicle, what helps prevent off-tracking into other lanes or the curb?

options.text: Turn from the far-left lane without signaling
Swing wide as needed while keeping the rear of the vehicle clear of hazards
Cut the corner tightly to stay in one lane
Accelerate through the turn without checking mirrors

explanation: Trailers off-track inside the tractor path; drivers must plan the turn, use mirrors, and swing appropriately without hitting curb or other traffic.

distractorExplanations.a: Wrong-lane turns and no signal are illegal/unsafe.
distractorExplanations.c: Cutting tight often curbs the trailer wheels.
distractorExplanations.d: Acceleration without mirror checks invites collisions.

prompt: What is hydroplaning most closely related to?

options.text: Engine oil temperature only
Trailer ABS malfunction only
Water buildup under tires reducing traction, worse with underinflation and higher speed
Parking brake spring strength

explanation: Hydroplaning occurs when tires ride on water; speed, tread depth, and inflation strongly affect risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Oil temperature is unrelated to hydroplaning.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS helps control skids but is not the cause of hydroplaning.
distractorExplanations.d: Parking brake springs do not cause hydroplaning.

prompt: When descending a long, steep grade, the preferred method to control speed is usually:

options.text: Ride the clutch continuously
Stay in the highest gear available
Turn off the engine to save fuel
Select a safe low gear before starting down and use proper braking technique

explanation: Choose a gear that holds speed without excessive braking; then use steady or snub braking as trained—not high gear or engine-off coasting.

distractorExplanations.a: Riding the clutch damages the clutch and reduces control.
distractorExplanations.b: High gear often leads to runaway speed.
distractorExplanations.c: Turning off the engine can lose power steering/brakes assist and is dangerous.

prompt: What should you do if you are being tailgated by another vehicle?

options.text: Increase your following distance ahead and avoid sudden moves; do not speed up just to please the tailgater
Brake hard immediately to warn them
Drive on the shoulder at full speed
Block both lanes so they cannot pass

explanation: Create more cushion ahead so you can brake gradually; aggressive braking or blocking escalates risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Hard brake-checking can cause a rear-end crash.
distractorExplanations.c: Shoulder driving at speed is unsafe.
distractorExplanations.d: Blocking lanes is aggressive and illegal in many jurisdictions.

prompt: At night, if an oncoming vehicle's high beams are blinding you, the best practice is to:

options.text: Stare into their headlights to judge distance
Look toward the right edge of your lane / road markings until they pass
Turn on your own high beams in retaliation
Close your eyes briefly

explanation: Looking to the right edge helps you stay in lane without being dazzled; retaliatory high beams worsen the hazard.

distractorExplanations.a: Staring into headlights worsens temporary blindness.
distractorExplanations.c: Retaliation high beams endanger everyone.
distractorExplanations.d: Closing your eyes removes all visual control.

prompt: When should you use four-way emergency flashers while driving?

options.text: Whenever you exceed the speed limit
Only when the ABS light is on
When you are moving unusually slowly or stopped as a hazard to traffic, per company/state rules
Whenever the radio is off

explanation: Flashers warn others of a hazard (breakdown, very slow travel). Misuse can confuse other drivers; follow local/company policy.

distractorExplanations.a: Speeding is not a reason to use flashers.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS warning needs diagnosis, not automatic flashers.
distractorExplanations.d: Radio status is irrelevant.

prompt: What is a 'space cushion' in commercial driving?

options.text: The foam in the driver's seat
The air gap in the brake chamber only
The distance between dual tires only
The open space you maintain around your vehicle to escape hazards

explanation: Managing space ahead, behind, and to the sides gives time to react to sudden stops, merges, and road hazards.

distractorExplanations.a: Seat foam is comfort, not space management.
distractorExplanations.b: Brake chambers are mechanical components.
distractorExplanations.c: Dual tire gap is a maintenance item, not the driving concept.

prompt: If your vehicle starts to skid due to overbraking, what is the general corrective action (non-ABS)?

options.text: Release the brakes to regain rolling traction, then steer and reapply gently as needed
Apply the parking brake hard
Accelerate to straighten the vehicle
Turn the wheel fully lock-to-lock repeatedly

explanation: Locked wheels cannot steer; releasing brakes restores rolling contact so you can steer and then brake properly.

distractorExplanations.b: Parking brake can worsen a skid.
distractorExplanations.c: Acceleration often worsens loss of control.
distractorExplanations.d: Wild steering inputs can induce a spin.

prompt: Why should you avoid driving in other drivers' blind spots for long periods?

options.text: It improves your fuel economy
They may change lanes into you without seeing your vehicle
It activates your ABS automatically
It is required by HOS rules

explanation: Large vehicles create large blind spots; lingering beside another vehicle increases sideswipe risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Blind-spot positioning does not improve MPG.
distractorExplanations.c: ABS is not controlled by lane position.
distractorExplanations.d: HOS governs hours, not blind-spot etiquette.

prompt: When following another vehicle in good conditions, a heavy CMV generally needs:

options.text: The same following distance as a passenger car
More following distance because stopping distance is longer
No following distance if ABS is equipped
Less distance because trucks brake better downhill

explanation: Greater mass and longer stopping distance require more space ahead than light vehicles.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: The same following distance as a passenger car
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: No following distance if ABS is equipped
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Less distance because trucks brake better downhill

prompt: What is a key hazard when backing a CMV?

options.text: Backing is always safer than pulling forward
Blind spots are large; use a helper and get out to look when needed
Mirrors eliminate all blind spots on modern trucks
Backing never requires checking the rear

explanation: CMVs have large blind areas; walk-arounds and spotters reduce backing crashes.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Backing is always safer than pulling forward
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Mirrors eliminate all blind spots on modern trucks
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Backing never requires checking the rear

prompt: Driving through water deep enough to reach hubs can:

options.text: Improve brake cooling permanently
Wet brakes and reduce braking effectiveness until they dry
Increase tire tread depth
Disable the need for downshifting

explanation: Water can soak brake linings; test brakes gently after water crossings and dry them if needed.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Improve brake cooling permanently
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Increase tire tread depth
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Disable the need for downshifting

prompt: When another vehicle is in your blind spot, you should:

options.text: Assume they see you and merge anyway
Avoid lane changes until you confirm the space is clear
Honk continuously for 30 seconds
Speed up without signaling

explanation: Never change lanes into an occupied blind spot; wait until you can see or verify clearance.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Assume they see you and merge anyway
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Honk continuously for 30 seconds
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Speed up without signaling

prompt: On slippery roads, which practice is most appropriate?

options.text: Increase speed to get through ice patches faster
Reduce speed, increase following distance, and avoid sudden steering or braking
Use only the trailer hand valve for all stops
Disable ABS so wheels can lock

explanation: Smooth inputs and lower speed help maintain traction when friction is reduced.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Increase speed to get through ice patches faster
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Use only the trailer hand valve for all stops
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Disable ABS so wheels can lock

prompt: What is the main purpose of cargo securement rules?

options.text: To prevent cargo from shifting or falling and causing crashes or roadway hazards
To increase axle weight beyond legal limits
To reduce the need for pre-trip inspections
To replace bill of lading requirements

explanation: Securement keeps loads stable under acceleration, braking, and turns so cargo does not spill or destabilize the vehicle.

distractorExplanations.b: Securement does not legalize overweight axles.
distractorExplanations.c: Inspections remain required.
distractorExplanations.d: Shipping papers are separate requirements.

prompt: Gross combination weight rating (GCWR) refers to:

options.text: Only the weight of the empty tractor
The maximum allowable weight of the power unit plus trailer(s) and cargo as rated
Only the trailer tire capacity
The driver's personal weight limit

explanation: GCWR is the manufacturer's rated maximum for the combination; operating above ratings is unsafe and often illegal.

distractorExplanations.a: Empty tractor weight is tare, not GCWR.
distractorExplanations.c: Tire ratings matter but GCWR is the combination rating.
distractorExplanations.d: Driver weight is not GCWR.

prompt: Placing too much weight on the steering axle can cause:

options.text: Automatic increase in fuel tank size
Elimination of trailer swing
Hard steering and possible tire overload / poor handling
Higher legal speed limits

explanation: Overloading the steer axle harms control and can exceed tire/axle ratings; proper weight distribution is essential.

distractorExplanations.a: Fuel capacity does not change with axle load.
distractorExplanations.b: Trailer swing is not eliminated by steer overload.
distractorExplanations.d: Speed limits are set by law, not axle load.

prompt: Why can a top-heavy load be dangerous?

options.text: It always reduces stopping distance
It improves ABS performance
It lowers bridge formula weight
It raises the center of gravity and increases rollover risk in turns

explanation: High CG makes rollovers more likely at the same speed in curves; load low and centered when possible.

distractorExplanations.a: Top-heavy loads often worsen stopping/handling, not improve them.
distractorExplanations.b: ABS cannot overcome physics of a high CG.
distractorExplanations.c: Bridge formula concerns axle spacing/weight, not CG height directly.

prompt: When covering a load with a tarp, a key securement principle is:

options.text: Secure tarp edges so wind cannot get under and tear the cover or unload cargo
Leave one side completely open for ventilation always
Use the tarp instead of required tiedowns
Tie the tarp only to the landing gear

explanation: Wind can balloon poorly secured tarps; covers must be fastened so they protect cargo without replacing required tiedowns.

distractorExplanations.b: Leaving a side open can peel the tarp.
distractorExplanations.c: Tarps do not replace tiedown working load requirements.
distractorExplanations.d: Landing gear alone is not a proper tarp anchor plan.

prompt: What does 'working load limit' (WLL) of a tiedown refer to?

options.text: The weight of the empty trailer
The maximum load the device is designed to secure under normal use
The driver's maximum driving hours
The tire ply rating only

explanation: WLL is used to ensure aggregate tiedown capacity meets cargo weight requirements under FMCSA securement rules.

distractorExplanations.a: Empty trailer weight is not WLL.
distractorExplanations.c: HOS is unrelated to tiedown WLL.
distractorExplanations.d: Tire ply is a separate rating.

prompt: If cargo shifts while you are driving, what should you do?

options.text: Increase speed to stabilize the load
Ignore it if the trailer doors are closed
Stop safely as soon as possible and resecure/rearrange before continuing
Brake hard from highway speed immediately in your lane

explanation: Shifted cargo changes handling and can spill; stop clear of traffic and fix securement.

distractorExplanations.a: Higher speed can worsen instability.
distractorExplanations.b: Closed doors do not mean the load is safe.
distractorExplanations.d: Panic braking risks jackknife/loss of control; get to a safe stop.

prompt: Bridge formula / axle spacing rules exist mainly to:

options.text: Increase maximum trailer length only
Replace CDL licensing
Eliminate the need for scales
Protect bridges and roadways by limiting concentrated axle loads

explanation: Federal bridge formula spreads weight over axle groups so bridges are not overloaded by short, heavy combinations.

distractorExplanations.a: Length rules are separate from bridge formula.
distractorExplanations.b: CDL licensing remains required.
distractorExplanations.c: Scales are still used for enforcement.

prompt: For liquid tankers, what is 'surge'?

options.text: Forward/backward liquid movement that can push the vehicle during stops and turns
A type of tire brand
Engine turbo lag only
ABS sensor noise

explanation: Liquid surge can shove the tractor during braking or cornering; baffles help but drivers must still manage speed and space.

distractorExplanations.b: Surge is a liquid dynamics concept, not a tire brand.
distractorExplanations.c: Turbo lag is engine response, not cargo surge.
distractorExplanations.d: ABS noise is unrelated.

prompt: Overloading a vehicle beyond its tire ratings can result in:

options.text: Improved wet traction always
Tire failure, fires, and loss of control
Longer tire life
Automatic legal overweight permits

explanation: Exceeding tire load ratings causes heat buildup and blowouts; permits do not override tire/manufacturer limits for safety.

distractorExplanations.a: Overload worsens traction and heat.
distractorExplanations.c: Overload shortens tire life.
distractorExplanations.d: Permits address legal weight, not tire safety ratings.

prompt: How often should cargo securement generally be rechecked on the road (beyond special exceptions)?

options.text: Never after leaving the dock
Within the first 50 miles and periodically thereafter as rules require
Only at state lines
Only if the shipper calls

explanation: Federal securement rules require early and periodic inspections because loads can shift.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Never after leaving the dock
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only at state lines
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Only if the shipper calls

prompt: Placing heavy cargo as low and centered as practical helps:

options.text: Increase the chance of rollover
Lower the center of gravity and improve stability
Bypass bridge formula limits
Eliminate the need for any tie-downs

explanation: A lower center of gravity reduces rollover risk and improves handling.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Increase the chance of rollover
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Bypass bridge formula limits
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Eliminate the need for any tie-downs

prompt: Which statement about working load limit (WLL) of tiedowns is correct?

options.text: WLL is irrelevant if the chain looks new
Aggregate WLL of tiedowns must meet required securement strength for the cargo
Any rope may substitute for rated tiedowns
WLL only applies to hazardous materials placards

explanation: Securement strength is based on rated WLL; inadequate capacity is a violation and a hazard.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: WLL is irrelevant if the chain looks new
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Any rope may substitute for rated tiedowns
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: WLL only applies to hazardous materials placards

prompt: Why can poorly distributed axle weights be a problem even if total GVW is legal?

options.text: Axle overloads can damage roads/bridges and create enforcement violations
Axle weights never matter under FMCSA rules
Only fuel tanks count toward axle weight
Overloading one axle always reduces stopping distance

explanation: Bridge formula and axle limits protect infrastructure; illegal axle loads can cite a legal GVW load.

distractorExplanations.b: Incorrect: Axle weights never matter under FMCSA rules
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only fuel tanks count toward axle weight
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Overloading one axle always reduces stopping distance

prompt: Covered/open loads that can blow or spill debris generally require:

options.text: No covering if traveling under 55 mph
Adequate covering or containment to prevent cargo loss onto the roadway
Only a verbal promise from the shipper
Placards instead of securement

explanation: Loose material must be contained so it does not endanger other road users.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: No covering if traveling under 55 mph
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only a verbal promise from the shipper
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Placards instead of securement

prompt: If you have a tire fire, what is generally the safest approach?

options.text: Get everyone away, call emergency services, and use proper extinguishing methods—do not try to drive long distances on a burning tire
Pour gasoline on the tire to smother flames
Drive at high speed to blow out the fire
Ignore it until the next rest area 50 miles away

explanation: Tire fires can rekindle and spread to cargo; evacuate, request help, and use appropriate firefighting guidance.

distractorExplanations.b: Gasoline feeds fire.
distractorExplanations.c: Driving fans flames and risks explosion/spread.
distractorExplanations.d: Delay allows the fire to grow.

prompt: What is the purpose of placing reflective triangles after a breakdown?

options.text: To measure following distance
To warn approaching traffic of a stopped vehicle hazard
To reset the ELD
To secure cargo inside the trailer

explanation: Advance warning devices give other drivers time to slow or change lanes around a disabled CMV.

distractorExplanations.a: Triangles are not for measuring following distance.
distractorExplanations.c: ELD reset is electronic, not triangle-related.
distractorExplanations.d: Triangles do not secure cargo.

prompt: Under FMCSA hours-of-service concepts, why are rest breaks and driving limits required?

options.text: To increase maximum vehicle weight
To replace pre-trip inspections
To reduce fatigue-related crashes
To eliminate the need for seat belts

explanation: HOS rules limit driving and require rest so drivers are alert; fatigue is a major crash factor.

distractorExplanations.a: HOS does not raise weight limits.
distractorExplanations.b: Inspections remain mandatory.
distractorExplanations.d: Seat belts remain required.

prompt: If your brakes fail on a downgrade, a key emergency technique is to:

options.text: Shut off the engine and remove the keys immediately while rolling
Pump the accelerator to rebuild air
Jump from the cab at highway speed
Look for an escape ramp or safe escape path and downshift if possible while using remaining braking carefully

explanation: Escape ramps are designed for runaway vehicles; maintain control, warn others, and use any remaining system carefully—never jump out.

distractorExplanations.a: Killing the engine can lose assist systems while still needing control.
distractorExplanations.b: Accelerator pumping does not rebuild brake air.
distractorExplanations.c: Jumping is almost always fatal.

prompt: What should you do if you are involved in a crash and not seriously injured?

options.text: Protect the scene, notify authorities, and exchange required information; help injured if trained/safe
Leave immediately to avoid paperwork
Move all vehicles into traffic lanes for photos
Admit full legal fault on the spot to speed things up

explanation: Post-crash duties include scene safety, notification, and required reports—without creating new hazards or improper admissions.

distractorExplanations.b: Leaving can be hit-and-run.
distractorExplanations.c: Blocking lanes unnecessarily increases secondary crashes.
distractorExplanations.d: Fault determination is for investigators/insurers; stick to facts.

prompt: Hazmat placards are required when:

options.text: Any consumer commodity is aboard regardless of quantity
The type and amount of hazardous material meet placarding thresholds in the regulations
The trailer is empty of all residue always
The driver holds only a Class C car license

explanation: Placarding depends on hazard class and quantity; drivers transporting placarded loads need proper CDL endorsements.

distractorExplanations.a: Not all consumer goods require placards.
distractorExplanations.c: Some empty/residue tanks still require placards.
distractorExplanations.d: Placarded hazmat typically needs CDL with hazmat endorsement.

prompt: What is a common requirement for emergency equipment on CMVs?

options.text: A spare engine only
Two spare steering wheels
Fire extinguisher and warning devices (e.g., triangles) meeting regulatory specs
A passenger airbag in the sleeper only

explanation: Federal rules require specific emergency equipment so drivers can warn traffic and fight small fires.

distractorExplanations.a: Spare engines are not required emergency equipment.
distractorExplanations.b: Spare steering wheels are not standard required gear.
distractorExplanations.d: Sleeper airbags are not the emergency equipment rule.

prompt: If you must stop on the shoulder due to a breakdown at night, you should:

options.text: Leave all lights off to save batteries
Stand in the travel lane to flag traffic
Park without warnings if you will fix it in under an hour
Use flashers, place triangles at required distances, and stay visible/safe

explanation: Visibility and advance warning protect you and other road users; never stand in traffic lanes.

distractorExplanations.a: Lights off make you invisible.
distractorExplanations.b: Standing in lanes is extremely dangerous.
distractorExplanations.c: Duration does not remove warning requirements.

prompt: Railroad crossing safety for CMVs includes which principle?

options.text: Never race a train; stop when required and do not shift gears while crossing
Always stop on the tracks if traffic ahead is heavy
High gear is required on all crossings
Trains can always stop quicker than trucks

explanation: Trains cannot swerve/stop quickly; never stop on tracks, and avoid gear changes that could stall on the crossing.

distractorExplanations.b: Stopping on tracks is a critical error.
distractorExplanations.c: Proper gear is one that lets you cross without shifting, not necessarily highest.
distractorExplanations.d: Trains need long distances to stop.

prompt: What does 'out-of-service' mean when an inspector places a vehicle OOS?

options.text: The vehicle gets a free pass for 24 hours
The vehicle/driver may not operate until the violation is corrected
Only the trailer lights need cleaning
The driver must increase speed to the terminal

explanation: OOS orders prohibit operation until defects or driver violations are fixed per inspection criteria.

distractorExplanations.a: There is no free-pass interpretation.
distractorExplanations.c: OOS covers serious safety violations, not cosmetic cleaning only.
distractorExplanations.d: Speeding to terminal while OOS is illegal.

prompt: If you feel drowsy while driving a CMV, the safest choice is to:

options.text: Open the window and keep driving through the night
Stop at a safe place and rest before continuing
Drink coffee and increase speed to arrive sooner
Use cruise control so you can close your eyes briefly

explanation: Fatigue is a major CMV crash factor; only sleep/rest restores alertness reliably.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Open the window and keep driving through the night
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Drink coffee and increase speed to arrive sooner
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Use cruise control so you can close your eyes briefly

prompt: What should you do if your tires blow out on a steer axle?

options.text: Slam on the brakes immediately and turn hard
Hold the wheel firmly, ease off the accelerator, and steer to a safe stop
Accelerate to straighten the truck
Shift to neutral and leave the roadway uncontrolled

explanation: Sudden hard braking can worsen loss of control; grip, stabilize, then stop safely.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Slam on the brakes immediately and turn hard
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Accelerate to straighten the truck
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Shift to neutral and leave the roadway uncontrolled

prompt: A CDL disqualification can result from:

options.text: Only crashes that occur in a personal car
Serious traffic offenses and certain railroad-crossing or alcohol/drug violations in a CMV
Having too many preventive maintenance records
Using engine brakes on a downgrade

explanation: Major and serious offenses (and other listed violations) can disqualify a CDL.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Only crashes that occur in a personal car
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Having too many preventive maintenance records
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Using engine brakes on a downgrade

prompt: When must you stop before crossing railroad tracks in a CMV (typical rule for buses/placarded loads)?

options.text: Never; CMVs always have the right-of-way
When required by signs, signals, or vehicle/cargo type rules that mandate a stop
Only if the crossing has no lights
Only during fog

explanation: Certain CMVs must stop at crossings; all drivers must obey active signals and avoid stalled tracks.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Never; CMVs always have the right-of-way
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only if the crossing has no lights
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Only during fog

prompt: If your vehicle starts to skid, a general recovery principle is to:

options.text: Steer into the skid and avoid overcorrecting while reducing demand on traction
Close your eyes and lock all brakes
Downshift forcibly through every gear
Turn off the engine immediately while moving

explanation: Countersteer appropriately and restore traction gradually; abrupt inputs worsen skids.

distractorExplanations.b: Incorrect: Close your eyes and lock all brakes
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Downshift forcibly through every gear
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Turn off the engine immediately while moving
